text: The surgeon inserts the first port in the left subcostal region and performs scope dissection, using the robotic camera to create the initial surgical space.
The surgeon inserts the first port under direct vision and uses a bipolar cutter to create additional space, allowing for the placement of two more robotic ports.
The video shows the posterior rectus sheath on the floor and the rectus abdominis on the roof, highlighting the anatomical structures visible during the procedure.

The video shows the medial edge where the anterior rectus sheath (ARS) and posterior rectus sheath (PRS) meet, with the linea seminalis visible laterally. The retromuscular space is clearly visible in the center.
The surgeon prepares to complete the dissection of the left retromuscular space using robotic instruments, focusing on precise foot placements for the robotic system.
The surgeon uses G spinal needles to determine the direction and depth for placing the robotic port, carefully avoiding injury to the left-sided inferior epigastric vessels.
The surgeon clears the attachment of the posterior rectus sheath from the anterior rectus sheath, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon operates within a confined space of approximately seven centimeters, maneuvering the camera and instruments to perform dissection and create space while entering the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon identifies the junction of the posterior rectus sheath and the anterior rectus sheath. The next step involves dividing the posterior rectus sheath approximately three millimeters below this junction.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to avoid opening the peritoneum, ensuring the surgical space remains uncompromised during the procedure.
The surgeon uses backward strokes with scissors and electrocautery to divide the bridge of tissue, widening the surgical space.
The surgeon continues to clear the left retromuscular space while dividing the posterior rectus sheath, taking care not to divide the peritoneum or enter the cavity.

The surgeon performs further dissection on the caudal side, with the left side oriented caudally and the right side cranially. The surgical ports are positioned laterally on the left side, establishing the orientation of the surgical field.
The cut edge of the posterior rectus sheath is visible, with the peritoneum lying beyond it, abutting the thinned-out linea alba.

The surgeon carefully preserves a small layer of the linea alba on the peritoneum, noting the thinness of the peritoneum in this patient. This strategic preservation is intended to avoid complications during closure of the posterior elements.
The surgeon continues the dissection, carefully peeling the peritoneum away from the infraumbilical divarication, marking the end of this segment of the procedure.
The surgeon shifts focus cranially, performing a backward movement to divide the posterior rectus sheath (PRS) and any intervening tissue between the existing incision and a new one.

The camera shows the thinned-out peritoneum on the floor, appearing as a translucent membrane separating the abdominal contents. Above, the divarication of the recti is visible, showing the thinned-out connection between the two rectus abdominis muscles, which is planned for repair.

The central area of the screen shows the hernia, with the surgeon carefully dissecting around it without directly manipulating the hernia itself, due to the thinness of the peritoneum and hernia site.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection, including caudal dissection, around the hernia site. The dissection progresses as high as necessary to facilitate closure or apposition of the defects, potentially requiring a partial transversus abdominis release (TAR).

The hernia presents a volcano-like appearance, protruding prominently. The surgeon performs circumferential dissections around the hernia, carefully separating surrounding tissues to expose the hernia sac.

The surgeon focuses on the posterior rectus sheath on the right side, identified by the orientation of its fibers. The incision is developed cranially and caudally to match the dissection performed on the left side, with attention to the patient's specific needs.

The surgeon is dissecting the loose areolar tissue in the right-sided retro muscular space, creating a space between two abdominal layers. The dissection continues cranially until reaching an area where the inter-rectus distance is approximately two to three centimeters.

The surgeon continues the dissection, extending cranially to create a large space on the right side. The focus then shifts to the left-sided posterior rectus sheath at the cranial end, preparing the area for robotic assistance.

The surgeon demonstrates the preperitoneal space creation in the center and on the right side, revealing a clear and open surgical field. The robotic instruments are positioned to facilitate precise and minimally invasive cutting and entry.

The surgeon completes the creation of a large space by connecting the right and left retro muscular spaces with the midline preperitoneal space. These distinct anatomical spaces are united to form a common large space, visible in the anterior abdominal wall, where prostheses will be placed for reinforcement.

The surgeon focuses on dissecting the remaining tissue hanging from the roof, identified as the hernia. Efforts are made to preserve as much peritoneum as possible during the dissection, moving from the hernia towards the floor.

The surgeon prepares to close the defect in a tension-free manner, focusing on achieving a safe posterior closure. The peritoneum appears thinned out, and the surgeon begins cutting and dividing it from the skin and subcutaneous tissue to avoid creating a hole in the skin.

The surgeon peels the peritoneum away from the skin and subcutaneous tissue, focusing on the last bit of tissue holding on the right side. This step visually resembles the separation of the sac and pseudo sac.

The surgeon completes the dissection, successfully positioning all posterior elements on the floor with minimal tissue loss. The surgical field shows a large, unified space, indicating significant progress in the procedure.

The surgeon measures the created space with a scale and places a large prosthesis to cover the entire hernia and repair the divarication of recti. The focus is on closing the hernial defect without opposing the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon clears fibro fatty tissue from the infraumbilical area, revealing the right-sided inferior epigastric vessels. Further lateral dissection is performed, carefully avoiding the neurovascular bundles visible from the floor to the roof, ensuring their preservation with robotic precision.

The surgeon completes the dissection by removing a small remaining piece of tissue. The umbilical hernia is visible, and the divarication has been stripped of the peritoneum. The surgeon begins planning the closure of the herniated defect and placation of the divarication.

The surgeon uses a barbed suture for closure, employing unidirectional sutures with needle drivers and scissors. The camera is inverted, making the anteriorly placed divarication of recti appear as if it is being sutured on the floor of the surgical field.

The surgeon takes a small bite from the subcutaneous tissue to reduce seroma formation, followed by conventional suturing of the supraumbilical component using backhand suturing. A horizontal mattress technique is employed for a challenging segment near the port to ensure strong tissue apposition. Suturing continues towards the umbilical defect with slight overlap for a tight repair.

The surgeon closes the defect in the posterior elements, including the PRS and peritoneum. A scale is used to measure the dissected cavity. A tailored mesh, either 20x15 or 25x25, is trimmed and placed flat in the cavity. The cavity is gradually deflated and re-inflated to ensure the mesh is properly positioned, marking the end of the surgery.
